target.title: fix(message-format): honor caller --limit for read, pins and search
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `renderMessageList` in `src/commands/message-format.ts` truncated CLI output with a hardcoded `messages.slice(0, 25)`. The CLI exposed `--limit` for `message read`, `message pins`, and Discord `message search`, and providers honored the upstream limit, but the rendered table still capped at 25 rows. Provider pagination signals (`hasMore`, `nextBatch`, `@odata.nextLink`) were also discarded, and Discord's read action returns no `hasMore` field at all so a full page gave no signal that older history existed. - Why it matters: callers requesting `--limit 50` saw 25 rows with no indication more existed. The maintainer review on #71452 (steipete + clawsweeper) explicitly asked for this contract fix. - What changed: `renderMessageList` now takes `displayLimit` from `FormatOpts` and returns `{ lines, total, displayed, limit }`. `formatMessageCliText` accepts an optional `{ displayLimit }`. The CLI parses `--limit` via the existing `parsePositiveIntOrUndefined` helper. A new `renderPaginationHint` emits a "Showing X of Y; raise --limit to see more" line when the payload had more rows than were rendered, "More results available beyond this page; use --json for the raw cursor" when the payload exposes `hasMore` / `nextBatch` / `@odata.nextLink`, and a heuristic "Reached --limit (N); raise it to fetch older history if any" when the provider returned exactly the requested limit and no other signal is available. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no provider-side changes. MS Teams `list-pins` still ignores caller `limit` upstream, MS Teams `channel-list` still has no exposed cursor, and Feishu pagination is untouched. The default 25-row cap is preserved when `--limit` is absent. JSON payloads are unchanged. The hint deliberately avoids prescribing `--before` / `--after` because those flags only exist on `message read` (not `pins`/`search`) and take message ids rather than the raw cursor tokens returned by Matrix/Graph. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #71452 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: `message read --limit N`, `message pins --limit N`, and Discord `message search --limit N` truncated to 25 rows; full Discord pages gave no hint that older history existed. - Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux WSL2, Node 22, pnpm 10, against a Discord channel with thousands of historical messages. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: - `pnpm build` - `pnpm openclaw message read --target channel: --channel discord --limit 50` -> renders 50 rows + "Reached --limit (50); raise it to fetch older history if any" - `pnpm openclaw message read --target channel: --channel discord --limit 50 --before --after ` -> renders 50 rows + heuristic hint - `pnpm openclaw message read --target channel: --channel discord` -> renders 25 rows + "Showing 25 of 50; raise --limit to see more" (Discord returns 50 by default; we display 25) - `pnpm openclaw message read --target channel: --channel discord --limit 50 --after ` -> renders 3 rows, no hint (range exhausted) - Evidence after fix: terminal screenshot attached to this PR. Automated proof: 11/11 new formatter tests pass plus 5/5 existing message tests; oxfmt/oxlint/tsgo all clean. - Observed result after fix: rendered table honors `--limit`. Hint line varies based on signal: truncation when `total > displayed`, cursor when payload exposes pagination metadata, heuristic when the page filled exactly to `--limit`. No false positives when the range was exhausted (3 < limit). - What was not tested: live MS Teams Graph `@odata.nextLink` and Matrix `nextBatch` cursor branches against real provider sessions. Both branches are covered in `src/commands/message-format.test.ts` against representative payload shapes. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): same command on `main` rendered 25 rows with no hint line; the cap was deterministic from the literal `slice(0, 25)`. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `renderMessageList` in `src/commands/message-format.ts` had a hardcoded `messages.slice(0, 25)` predating the `--limit` CLI option; the limit was never threaded into the renderer. - Missing detection / guardrail: no formatter-level test asserted that `--limit > 25` actually produces more than 25 rendered rows. Existing tests asserted JSON payload shapes, not rendered text. - Contributing context (if known): provider plugins added their own `--limit` and cursor handling over time but the shared CLI renderer was never updated to match. Pagination metadata (`hasMore`, `nextBatch`, `@odata.nextLink`) was already populated by Slack/Matrix/MS Teams but consumed nowhere; Discord returned no metadata at all, so a full page silently looked the same as a complete page. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `src/commands/message-format.test.ts`. - Scenario the test should lock in: `formatMessageCliText` honors `displayLimit` for `read`, `list-pins`, and `search`; default behavior unchanged when no limit is passed; pagination hint appears for `hasMore`, `nextBatch`, `@odata.nextLink`, when payload exceeds the requested limit, and when the provider returned exactly `--limit` rows; no false hint when the page is partial. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is at the formatter contract (one shared file across all three actions). A formatter-level unit test executes the same code path the CLI exercises and is fast and deterministic. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): none. The existing `src/commands/message.test.ts` covers config plumbing, not rendered output. - If no new test is added, why not: n/a (added). ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `openclaw message read --limit N`, `openclaw message pins --limit N`, and `openclaw message search --limit N` now render up to N rows instead of always capping at 25. Default remains 25 when `--limit` is omitted. - A muted hint line appears below the rendered table when: - the source payload had more rows than were displayed: `Showing X of Y; raise --limit to see more` - the provider returned `hasMore: true`, a Matrix `nextBatch`, or an MS Teams Graph `@odata.nextLink`: `More results available beyond this page; use --json for the raw cursor` - the provider returned exactly the requested `--limit` rows and no other signal exists: `Reached --limit (N); raise it to fetch older history if any` - JSON output (`--json`) is unchanged and still carries the raw cursor fields. ## Diagram (if applicable) n/a ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: n/a ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Linux WSL2 (Ubuntu) - Runtime/container: Node 22, pnpm 10 - Model/provider: n/a (rendering only) - Integration/channel (if any): tested live against a Discord channel with thousands of historical messages - Relevant config (redacted): n/a ### Steps 1. `pnpm build` 2. `pnpm openclaw message read --target channel: --channel discord --limit 50` (full-page heuristic) 3. `pnpm openclaw message read --target channel: --channel discord` (default-cap truncation) 4. `pnpm openclaw message read --target channel: --channel discord --limit 50 --after ` (partial page; no hint) ### Expected - Step 2 renders 50 rows + "Reached --limit (50); raise it to fetch older history if any". - Step 3 renders 25 rows + "Showing 25 of 50; raise --limit to see more" when Discord returned 50. - Step 4 renders fewer than 50 rows with no hint. - On `main` step 3 renders 25 rows with no hint regardless. ### Actual - Matches expected. Screenshot attached to the PR. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) The new formatter tests in `src/commands/message-format.test.ts` would have failed against the prior `slice(0, 25)` cap (e.g. `expect(countRowsInOutput(out, 30)).toBe(30)` for `read` with `displayLimit: 30`). ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: ran `message read --limit 50` against a live Discord channel with hundreds of messages and confirmed 50 rows + "Reached --limit (50)" hint; ran `message read` without `--limit` and confirmed "Showing 25 of 50; raise --limit to see more" hint; ran `message read --limit 50 --after ` and confirmed partial page renders without a false-positive hint. - Edge cases checked: invalid `--limit` values (`0`, `-3`, non-numeric strings) fall through `parsePositiveIntOrUndefined` to `undefined`, which the formatter treats as "use default 25"; payload smaller than the requested limit renders all rows with no hint; provider cursor branches (`hasMore`, `nextBatch`, `@odata.nextLink`) covered by unit tests against representative payload shapes. - What I did not verify: live Matrix `nextBatch` and live MS Teams Graph `@odata.nextLink` cursor branches against real provider sessions. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: n/a ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: the full-page heuristic can produce a "Reached --limit" hint even when the user happened to request exactly the number of remaining messages. - Mitigation: the wording says "raise it to fetch older history if any", framing it as a possibility rather than a fact. Raising `--limit` quickly verifies. - Risk: rendering very large payloads when the caller passes a huge `--limit`. - Mitigation: caller-controlled, providers already enforce upstream caps in their fetch layer; the formatter only renders what the provider returned. - Risk: pagination hint phrasing diverges from existing CLI hint dialects elsewhere in the codebase (`sessions.ts`, `status.command-sections.ts`). - Mitigation: phrasing is muted-styled and additive; consolidating shared hint helpers is a separate follow-up. ## Test plan - pnpm test src/commands/message-format.test.ts src/commands/message.test.ts - pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/message-format.ts src/commands/message-format.test.ts src/commands/message.ts CHANGELOG.md docs/cli/message.md - node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/commands/message-format.ts src/commands/message-format.test.ts src/commands/message.ts - pnpm tsgo:all - pnpm build && pnpm openclaw message read --target channel: --channel discord --limit 50 ## Before / After Before: - `openclaw message read --limit 50` rendered 25 rows even when the provider returned 50. - Provider pagination signals (`hasMore`, `nextBatch`, `@odata.nextLink`) were silently discarded by the CLI renderer. - Discord's full-page reads gave no signal that older history existed. After: - The CLI renders up to `--limit` rows for `read`, `list-pins`, and `search`. Default stays at 25 when `--limit` is omitted. - A muted "Showing X of Y; raise --limit to see more" hint appears under the table when the payload had more rows than were displayed. - A generic "More results available beyond this page; use --json for the raw cursor" hint appears when the provider returns `hasMore`, a Matrix `nextBatch`, or an MS Teams Graph `@odata.nextLink`. - A heuristic "Reached --limit (N); raise it to fetch older history if any" hint appears when the provider returned exactly the requested `--limit` rows and no other signal exists (covers Discord, which returns no `hasMore`). ## Bugfix trigger User invokes `openclaw message read --target --limit 50` (or `message pins --limit N`, or Discord `message search --limit N`) against a channel/source that returns more than 25 messages. Before this fix, the rendered table always stops at 25 rows regardless of the requested limit, and any `hasMore`/`nextBatch`/`@odata.nextLink` returned by the provider is hidden. For Discord specifically, even after raising `--limit` the user has no way to tell whether the page is exhaustive or partial because Discord doesn't return a `hasMore` signal.

target.title: docs(secrets): document POSIX allowInsecurePath behavior
target.body:
## Summary
- Document that SecretRef file and exec provider paths on POSIX normally must be owned by the gateway user uid.
- Clarify that trusted root-owned system-package binaries may need allowInsecurePath: true.
- Keep the warning that allowInsecurePath should only be used for trusted paths and that trustedDirs still applies to resolved symlink targets.

Fixes #78537

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: The SecretRef docs now explain the POSIX uid ownership check and the trusted root-owned binary case for allowInsecurePath.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.4.1 arm64 with Node 24.14.0 against a local OpenClaw checkout.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: rg -n POSIX systems|gateway user uid|root-owned docs/gateway/secrets.md docs/cli/secrets.md docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md
- Evidence after fix: terminal output excerpt:
  docs/cli/secrets.md:151: On POSIX systems, provider paths normally must be owned by the gateway user uid; root-owned system-package binaries such as /usr/bin/op or /usr/bin/sops can fail this check.
  docs/gateway/secrets.md:170: On POSIX systems, the path must normally be owned by the gateway user uid.
  docs/gateway/secrets.md:179: On POSIX systems, the command path must normally be owned by the gateway user uid, so root-owned package-manager binaries such as /usr/bin/op or /usr/bin/sops can require allowInsecurePath: true.
  docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md:892: On POSIX systems, provider paths normally must be owned by the gateway user uid, so root-owned system-package binaries can also fail verification.
- Observed result after fix: The CLI secrets guide, gateway secrets guide, and configuration reference all describe the POSIX uid ownership behavior and trusted root-owned binary guidance.
- What was not tested: Browser-rendered docs preview; this is a text-only docs update.

## Validation
- pnpm docs:list
- pnpm format:docs:check
- pnpm docs:check-mdx
- git diff --check
- GitHub Actions check-docs passed
- GitHub Actions Real behavior proof passed

target.title: fix(tts/xiaomi): support Token Plan TTS endpoint
target.body:
## Summary

Fix Xiaomi MiMo TTS for Token Plan users by:

- sending Xiaomi TTS requests with `Authorization: Bearer <key>` instead of the `api-key` header
- reusing `models.providers.xiaomi.baseUrl` for Xiaomi TTS when `messages.tts.providers.xiaomi.baseUrl` is not configured
- preserving explicit TTS `baseUrl` overrides for users who need speech to use a different endpoint
- documenting Token Plan regional endpoints such as `token-plan-cn` and `token-plan-ams`

This handles the Token Plan failure mode where `tp-...` keys must call the matching regional Token Plan endpoint instead of the default pay-as-you-go endpoint.

Closes #77692

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Xiaomi MiMo TTS with a Token Plan key should call the regional Token Plan endpoint and synthesize audio successfully instead of returning `401 Invalid API Key` from the default pay-as-you-go endpoint.

**Real environment tested:** Local Mac mini OpenClaw checkout using a real Xiaomi MiMo Token Plan key against `https://token-plan-cn.xiaomimimo.com/v1`. The key was kept local and was not pasted into the PR, issue, logs, or code.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
set +x
export XIAOMI_API_KEY="<redacted real Token Plan key>"
export XIAOMI_BASE_URL="https://token-plan-cn.xiaomimimo.com/v1"
export OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1
npx pnpm@10.33.2 vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.live.config.ts extensions/xiaomi/xiaomi.live.test.ts
unset XIAOMI_API_KEY XIAOMI_BASE_URL OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST
```

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal output from the local run, with secrets redacted:

```text
Result: PASS
baseUrl: token-plan-cn
Command: npx pnpm@10.33.2 vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.live.config.ts extensions/xiaomi/xiaomi.live.test.ts
Tests: 2 passed
Endpoint: https://token-plan-cn.xiaomimimo.com/v1
Confirmed default endpoint was not used: https://api.xiaomimimo.com/v1

Passed live cases:
1. synthesizes MiMo TTS through the registered speech provider - 2.1s
2. synthesizes MiMo TTS as an Opus voice note - 13.1s
```

**Observed result after fix:** Both live Xiaomi TTS paths succeeded with the real Token Plan endpoint: MP3 audio-file synthesis and Opus voice-note synthesis. The run used `token-plan-cn` and did not hit the default pay-as-you-go endpoint.

**What was not tested:** `token-plan-ams` was not live-tested in this run. Unit coverage verifies `models.providers.xiaomi.baseUrl` inheritance and explicit TTS `baseUrl` precedence; the live run used `XIAOMI_BASE_URL` to target `token-plan-cn`.

## Verification

- `npx pnpm@10.33.2 test extensions/xiaomi/speech-provider.test.ts`
  - 14 passed
- `npx pnpm@10.33.2 exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/providers/xiaomi.md docs/tools/tts.md extensions/xiaomi/speech-provider.ts extensions/xiaomi/speech-provider.test.ts`
  - passed
- `git diff --check`
  - passed
- Live Token Plan CN verification from the local Mac mini environment:
  - `npx pnpm@10.33.2 vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.live.config.ts extensions/xiaomi/xiaomi.live.test.ts`
  - 2 passed
  - endpoint: `https://token-plan-cn.xiaomimimo.com/v1`
  - confirmed the live run did not use `https://api.xiaomimimo.com/v1`

`npx pnpm@10.33.2 check:changed` passed the extension production typecheck but failed in the broader extension test typecheck on unrelated existing test files under `extensions/diffs`, `extensions/irc`, `extensions/line`, `extensions/nextcloud-talk`, and `extensions/qqbot`.

target.title: [codex] Cron: repair empty sanitized final replies
target.body:
﻿???## Summary

- Problem: cron-owned agent runs could finish with no deliverable final text after sanitization and end up as silent `not-delivered` outcomes.
- Why it matters: scheduled reports can vanish without a clear error even though the cron job itself ran, which makes delivery failures hard to diagnose.
- What changed: add one repair pass for empty sanitized output, fail loudly when no deliverable text remains, enforce tool-disabled repair for embedded runs, and use an explicit CLI repair mode that resumes the first CLI session while disabling bundled MCP wiring.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not relax reasoning-tag sanitization, does not change normal non-cron agent turns, and does not address the legacy local WhatsApp health-check cron issue tracked separately in #60204.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Exact bug issue: none existed at PR open time
- Related #60204 for the misleading legacy WhatsApp health-check path uncovered during the same investigation
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: cron delivery could receive an empty final text after sanitization (for example when malformed reasoning tags consumed the visible reply), but the cron runner had no recovery pass and the delivery path did not convert that condition into a hard error.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the runner did not retry with a constrained no-tools finalization prompt, and the direct-delivery path treated missing synthesized text too softly.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the existing sanitization behavior is intentional; the gap was in cron finalization and delivery handling, not in the sanitizer itself.
- Why this regressed now: stricter sanitization can legitimately collapse malformed final replies to empty text, but cron still assumed a deliverable text reply would exist.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out transport-only failure for the investigated case; the actionable gap here was the empty-output cron path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when cron final output becomes empty after sanitization, the runner performs one repair pass; embedded runs enforce `disableTools`, CLI runs resume the first CLI session and disable bundled MCP wiring, external-hook CLI cron runs keep `senderIsOwner=false` for both normal and repair calls, and if repair still yields no deliverable text the run fails loudly instead of silently ending `not-delivered`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in cron run finalization plus delivery dispatch, and the narrowest stable guardrail is to exercise those exact seams with mocked agent output.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing interim-retry coverage already covered acknowledgement retries but not empty sanitized output.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron jobs that lose their visible final text after sanitization now get one repair attempt before delivery.
- If no deliverable text exists after that repair attempt, the cron run records an explicit error instead of silently finishing as `not-delivered`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [delivery path sees no text] -> [silent not-delivered]

After:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [no-tools repair pass]
          -> [recovered text -> deliver] or [still empty -> explicit cron error]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the embedded repair path enforces `disableTools`; the CLI repair path is explicitly not represented as native-tool-free, resumes the first CLI session, and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring. The risk is limited to cron finalization behavior, and dedicated regression tests cover both embedded and CLI-backed paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: mocked embedded + CLI runner paths in targeted tests
- Integration/channel (if any): cron + Telegram announce path in regression coverage
- Relevant config (redacted): default test fixtures

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`
2. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent.skips-delivery-without-whatsapp-recipient-besteffortdeliver-true.test.ts`
3. Run `pnpm test -- src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`

### Expected

- Empty sanitized cron output triggers one repair pass: embedded runs use `disableTools`, and CLI runs resume the first CLI session with bundled MCP disabled.
- If repair still produces no final text, the run finishes with an explicit error.
- CLI-backed repair mode does not inject bundled MCP overlays.

### Actual

- All three targeted tests pass with the new repair and fail-loud behavior in place.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the cron finalization and delivery diff, ran the targeted regression tests locally, confirmed CLI repair resumes the first CLI session, confirmed external-hook CLI cron runs preserve non-owner sender scope for normal and repair calls, and confirmed the local changed-file check stack passed.
- Edge cases checked: empty payloads without descendants, repair success, repair failure, CLI repair-mode bundled MCP disablement, and first CLI session reuse during repair.
- What you did **not** verify: live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery against a real gateway session.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Cron-owned CLI agent runs that produce no deliverable final text now perform one repair pass and record the repaired final text instead of silently ending without a user-visible result. For CLI-backed cron runs, the repair pass resumes the first CLI session and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring without claiming native CLI tools are unavailable.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout. The live repair proof below was run at repair head `0f1b25fbfc`; current pushed head `d6ed463c96` keeps that repair path, additionally restores external-hook CLI non-owner sender scope, includes the upstream lint repair needed for the final CI head, and addresses the Greptile duplicate-descendant-predicate review note; verified with targeted red/green tests plus `pnpm lint --threads=8`. Local OpenClaw Gateway was at `ws://127.0.0.1:19162` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`; registered `codex-cli` runtime used installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5` through a thin proof wrapper around `codex exec --output-last-message`; cron delivery was disabled intentionally with `--no-deliver`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron add --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --name "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof" --at "2030-01-08T00:00:00Z" --keep-after-run --session isolated --message "Real behavior proof for PR 60212. If the prompt you are reading contains the phrase Repair it now, reply exactly OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK. Otherwise return an empty final answer and do not output any visible text." --no-deliver --timeout-seconds 300 --model openai/gpt-5.5 --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000

corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "eventLoop": { "degraded": false, "reasons": [] },
  "plugins": { "loaded": ["acpx", "browser", "canvas", "device-pair", "file-transfer", "memory-core", "openai", "phone-control", "talk-voice"], "errors": [] }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000
{
  "ok": true,
  "enqueued": true,
  "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2"
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "id": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
  "name": "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof",
  "delivery": { "mode": "none", "channel": "last" },
  "state": {
    "lastRunStatus": "ok",
    "lastStatus": "ok",
    "lastDurationMs": 14308,
    "lastDeliveryStatus": "not-requested",
    "consecutiveErrors": 0
  }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
{
  "entries": [
    {
      "jobId": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
      "action": "finished",
      "status": "ok",
      "summary": "OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK",
      "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2",
      "durationMs": 14308,
      "model": "gpt-5.5",
      "provider": "codex-cli",
      "delivered": false,
      "deliveryStatus": "not-requested"
    }
  ],
  "total": 1,
  "hasMore": false
}
```

Observed result after fix: The real local gateway accepted the cron job, ran it through the registered `codex-cli` backend, recovered `OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK` during the repair flow, and recorded the run as `status: "ok"`. Delivery was not requested by design because the proof command used `--no-deliver`; the current code keeps the CLI mode explicit, resumes the first CLI session for repair, and does not hit the earlier native-tool fail-closed error.

What was not tested: External WhatsApp/Telegram send was not exercised; delivery was disabled to keep the local proof isolated and non-invasive.
Current-head ownership, lint, and Greptile cleanup verification after commit `d6ed463c96`:

```text
# RED before the ownership fix:
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
FAIL passes senderIsOwner=false to external-hook CLI cron runs
AssertionError: expected true to be false

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
FAIL preserves external-hook non-owner scope during CLI empty-output repair
AssertionError: expected true to be false

# GREEN after the ownership fix:
$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       5 passed (5)

$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts

Tests       9 passed (9)

$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts

Tests       20 passed (20)

corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/cli-runner/types.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

corepack pnpm check:changed
$ node scripts/check-changed.mjs

# clean
```

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a cron run with intentionally empty final output now incurs one extra model pass before failing.
  - Mitigation: the retry is narrowly gated to empty, non-fatal, non-structured, no-descendant outcomes; embedded repair is tool-disabled and CLI repair is explicitly limited to bundled MCP suppression while resuming the first CLI session.
- Risk: CLI repair mode could accidentally preserve bundled MCP wiring.
  - Mitigation: `disableTools` now disables bundled MCP preparation, covered by `src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`.

target.title: docs(memory): add memory system deep dive guide
target.body:
## Summary

Add a comprehensive memory system deep dive guide, addressing #43032.

### Problem

`docs/concepts/memory.md` covers basics (~600 words) but lacks depth on memory best practices, search optimization, lifecycle management, and advanced patterns.

### New document: `docs/concepts/memory-deep-dive.md` (401 lines)

Covers all 6 sections requested in the issue:

1. **Memory Architecture** — Two-layer model (`MEMORY.md` + `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`), indexing scope, what is searchable vs not
2. **Writing Effective Memory** — What belongs in MEMORY.md vs daily logs, promotion workflow, formatting tips for better search indexing
3. **Search Optimization** — Hybrid retrieval pipeline (keyword + vector + MMR), query strategies (semantic vs exact), `memory_search` → `memory_get` workflow, tuning parameters (`maxResults`, `minScore`)
4. **Memory Maintenance** — Heartbeat-based periodic review, cleanup strategies, `heartbeat-state.json` tracking
5. **Advanced Patterns** — Entity tracking with stable headings, opinion evolution with timestamps, cross-session recall via `sessionMemory`
6. **Troubleshooting** — No results, keyword-only matches, `memory_get` file restrictions, MEMORY.md bloat, missing transcript search

### Navigation

Added to `docs.json` under Sessions and memory → Memory group, right after `concepts/memory`.

### Source references

- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.ts` — default settings (maxResults: 6, maxSnippetChars: 700)
- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/read-file.ts` — `memory_get` file boundary enforcement
- `src/agents/memory-search.ts` — search config resolution and sources
- `docs/concepts/memory-builtin.md` — existing builtin engine docs

Closes #43032

target.title: [codex] prefer terminal reply fields in CLI JSONL parser
target.body:
﻿﻿## Summary

- Problem: the CLI JSONL parser only special-cases Claude `type:"result"` events and otherwise falls back to generic text collection, so OpenAI/Codex-style fallback runs can lose terminal reply semantics.
- Why it matters: in channel-visible fallback paths, commentary/intermediate text can be surfaced instead of the actual final answer even when the JSONL stream already contains `phase:"final_answer"` and `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
- What changed: `parseCliJsonl()` now prefers `task_complete.last_agent_message`, then assistant messages tagged `phase:"final_answer"`, and skips `commentary`-phase generic item text.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Claude CLI handling is unchanged, no dispatch/channel code changed, and no provider failover logic changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #59918
- Related #25592
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `parseCliJsonl()` was written around Claude `type:"result"` lines plus a generic `parsed.item.text` fallback, but it did not understand newer OpenAI/Codex-style JSONL terminal fields.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no parser preference for `task_complete.last_agent_message` or `phase:"final_answer"`, and no guard against treating `commentary` item text as the outbound reply.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the current behavior is discussed in #59918 as a narrower parser-path issue adjacent to the broader channel-leak family in #25592.
- Why this regressed now: unknown as a specific regression point; this appears more like a compatibility gap exposed by fallback runs that emit richer JSONL event shapes.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: this fix does not depend on channel dispatching, failover selection, or Claude stream-json parsing.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when JSONL contains commentary, `final_answer`, and `task_complete`, the parser returns the terminal answer; when only `final_answer` is present, it wins over commentary text.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives entirely inside the JSONL parsing function and can be reproduced with synthetic line fixtures without needing a live provider or channel.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing Claude result tests in `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` cover the Claude-specific path and remain unchanged.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenAI/Codex-style CLI fallback runs now prefer terminal reply fields instead of surfacing commentary when both are present in JSONL.
- Commentary-only generic item text is no longer treated as the final reply in this parser path.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
CLI JSONL -> generic text collection -> commentary can win -> user sees wrong reply

After:
CLI JSONL -> task_complete / final_answer preferred -> terminal answer wins -> user sees final reply
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (local contributor checkout)
- Runtime/container: Node.js + pnpm
- Model/provider: parser coverage for OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output; Claude path rechecked via existing tests
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A for unit verification
- Relevant config (redacted): CLI backend `output: "jsonl"`

### Steps

1. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary events plus a terminal `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
2. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary plus assistant `phase:"final_answer"` content.
3. Run the targeted parser tests.

### Expected

- Terminal answer fields win over commentary.
- Claude JSONL result handling still passes unchanged.

### Actual

- Verified with targeted tests after the patch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/cli-output.test.ts src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts`
- Edge cases checked: existing Claude stream-json tests still pass; `task_complete` outranks `final_answer`; `final_answer` outranks commentary; commentary `item.text` is ignored.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not run a live WhatsApp/Telegram fallback end-to-end against a real CLI provider from this branch.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output is now parsed so terminal reply records win over commentary or intermediate message text in both the full-buffer parser and streaming parser.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout at PR head `8b90301ef3`; installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5`; Node `v24.13.1`; OpenClaw parser entry points imported directly from `src/agents/cli-output.ts`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK" 1> .artifacts/real-proof-59920/codex-ignore-rules.jsonl 2> .artifacts/real-proof-59920/codex-ignore-rules.stderr.txt

node --import tsx --input-type=module
imports parseCliJsonl() and createCliJsonlStreamingParser() from ./src/agents/cli-output.ts
feeds the saved Codex CLI JSONL transcript through both parser paths

node --import tsx --input-type=module
imports the same parser entry points and replays redacted Codex/OpenAI terminal-field JSONL containing commentary, final_answer, and task_complete records
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"
{"type":"thread.started","thread_id":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95"}
{"type":"turn.started"}
{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_0","type":"agent_message","text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"}}
{"type":"turn.completed","usage":{"input_tokens":22858,"cached_input_tokens":2432,"output_tokens":129,"reasoning_output_tokens":116}}

real_codex_full_buffer_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
real_codex_streaming_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
```

```text
terminal_field_jsonl=
{"type":"response_start","thread_id":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
{"item":{"type":"message","phase":"commentary","text":"COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN"}}
{"type":"response_item","payload":{"type":"message","role":"assistant","phase":"final_answer","content":[{"type":"output_text","text":"FINAL_ANSWER_SHOULD_BE_OVERRIDDEN"}]}}
{"type":"event_msg","payload":{"type":"task_complete","last_agent_message":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS"}}
full_buffer_output=
{"text":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS","sessionId":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
streaming_output=
{"text":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS","sessionId":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
```

Observed result after fix: The installed Codex CLI produced JSONL on this machine and both OpenClaw parser paths extracted the final agent message from that transcript. For the bot-cited terminal-field shape, both parser paths returned `TASK_COMPLETE_WINS`; `COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN` and the lower-priority `FINAL_ANSWER_SHOULD_BE_OVERRIDDEN` were not surfaced.

What was not tested: A live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery route was not exercised for this parser-only PR.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a non-Claude JSONL backend could intentionally rely on commentary-phase `item.text` as the only final output.
  - Mitigation: the parser now only skips explicit `commentary` phase text and prefers explicit terminal fields; generic non-commentary `item.text` fallback remains intact.

commands.review_summary:
This PR adds a C/GTK4/libadwaita Linux companion app under `apps/linux`, a GTK3 tray helper, native gateway/systemd/WebSocket/RPC/onboarding/management surfaces and tests, a Linux gateway client ID, and a small `agents.update` default-agent server fix.

Reproducibility: yes. for the review findings: source inspection shows cron edits always save `schedule_kind = "cron"`, session log lookup builds a key-based transcript path, and dashboard URLs interpolate raw hosts. I did not run the Linux app or its test suite in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] The remaining work is maintainer platform/security/product review plus conflict resolution; the code defects are concrete, but the overall PR is too broad and direction-sensitive for an automated repair lane.

target.title: feat: cute GTK-native Linux App (#75)
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: - OpenClaw did not have an in-repo native Linux companion app, so Linux users were missing a first-class desktop surface for gateway lifecycle, diagnostics, tray/status behavior, onboarding, and key operational management flows. - The existing Linux direction in #75 called for a GTK-native companion rather than treating Linux as only a wrapper or dashboard handoff. - Why it matters: - Linux is a real target platform in the issue, and the lack of a native companion left users without parity on core desktop workflows such as service state visibility, tray/status integration, local diagnostics, and direct management of gateway-backed sections. - Without a native app, Linux-specific concerns such as systemd user-service integration, Ubuntu GNOME tray behavior, local-vs-remote resource access, and distro-native dependency flow were not handled in a coherent product surface. - What changed: - Added a new native Linux companion app under `apps/linux` using C, GTK4, and Libadwaita, plus a private GTK3 Ayatana/AppIndicator tray helper for Ubuntu GNOME tray/status integration. - Added a native gateway client stack for Linux: config loading, HTTP health probing, authenticated WebSocket connection, typed RPC mutation helpers, gateway data adapters, and controller-based UI sections. - Added native operational management surfaces for Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances, including config-safe editing, locality-aware transcript/log opening, pairing actions, and richer management flows previously absent on Linux. - Added Linux runtime/readiness modeling, onboarding, diagnostics, notifications, systemd integration, and focused automated tests covering parser behavior, mutation request shapes, RPC lifecycle handling, config rebuild safety, runtime/readiness logic, and helper behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): - This PR does not add distro packaging artifacts such as `.deb`, `.rpm`, or Snap packages. - This PR does not attempt to solve the Windows side. - This PR does not claim full macOS parity across every desktop capability; it establishes the native Linux companion foundation and a substantial in-app management surface. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #75 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: - N/A - Missing detection / guardrail: - N/A - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - N/A - Why this regressed now: - N/A - If unknown, what was ruled out: - N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - N/A - Scenario the test should lock in: - N/A - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - N/A - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - N/A - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Linux now has a native GTK4 + Libadwaita companion app in-repo. - Ubuntu GNOME users get tray/status integration through the companion plus the private AppIndicator helper. - The Linux app can discover and work with the user `openclaw-gateway.service` lifecycle through native systemd integration. - The app surfaces first-run progression clearly across the main readiness states: - before `openclaw setup` - after `openclaw setup` but before gateway install - after gateway install with the service active and the gateway answering - The app surfaces runtime/readiness/onboarding/diagnostics states directly in the Linux UI. - Channels can now be inspected and managed in-app, including probe, logout, config editing, and WhatsApp QR linking flow. - Skills can now be filtered and managed in-app, including enable/disable, install, and env/API-key updates. - Sessions can now be managed in-app, including patch/reset/compact/delete and log access. - Cron can now be managed in-app, including scheduler status, recent runs, create/edit, run-now, enable/disable, delete, and transcript access. - Instances now expose richer remote node details, pending pairing approval/reject flows, and copy-debug-info behavior. - Log/transcript opening is locality-aware: local resources open locally when available, otherwise the UI falls back safely. - Channel config editing now validates edited JSON, preserves unrelated config keys, and saves using `baseHash`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user on Linux] -> [CLI / dashboard / partial prototype paths] -> [limited lifecycle visibility and little or no native management] After: [user on Linux] -> [native GTK4 companion app] -> [systemd + HTTP + authenticated WebSocket runtime model] -> [tray / diagnostics / onboarding / management sections] -> [native actions for channels, skills, sessions, cron, instances] ```` ## Security Impact (required) * New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: * The Linux companion introduces a new native desktop surface that can read local runtime context, inspect service state, connect to the local gateway over HTTP/WebSocket, edit config, open local logs/transcripts when available, and trigger gateway-backed management mutations. * Risk is mitigated by keeping the runtime model explicit, separating systemd discovery from gateway RPC transport, validating config JSON before save, preserving unrelated config content during rebuild, using `baseHash` on config writes, hardening request shapes through typed mutation helpers, and covering lifecycle/parsing/request-shape behavior in targeted tests. * Local-vs-remote resource access is handled explicitly so the app does not blindly assume every gateway-returned path is locally openable. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment * OS: * Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME * Runtime/container: * Bare-metal / local user-session workflow with systemd user service * Model/provider: * Local gateway-backed companion flow; model/provider depends on gateway config * Integration/channel (if any): * systemd user service, HTTP health, authenticated gateway WebSocket, GTK4 + Libadwaita, Ayatana/AppIndicator * Relevant config (redacted): * Fresh local OpenClaw config and user `openclaw-gateway.service`; secrets redacted ### Steps 1. Install Linux build dependencies and clone/build OpenClaw. 2. Build and run the Linux app with Meson. 3. Launch the Linux companion app before running `openclaw setup`. 4. Run `openclaw setup`, then relaunch or refresh the companion app. 5. Install and start the user gateway service. 6. Launch or refresh the Linux companion app again. 7. Exercise tray/status, diagnostics, onboarding/readiness, and the Dashboard / General / Channels / Skills / Sessions / Cron / Instances sections. 8. Run the Linux test suite. ### Expected * Before setup, the app reports the missing setup/runtime state clearly. * After setup but before gateway install, the app reports configured-but-not-installed service state clearly. * After gateway install/start, the app reports a healthy connected state, with diagnostics, dashboard, and general surfaces reflecting the running gateway. * The Linux app builds, launches, connects through the native runtime model, reflects systemd/gateway state accurately, and exposes native management flows for the major gateway-backed sections. * The Linux test suite passes. ### Actual * Matches expected in local verification. ## Evidence Attach at least one: * [x] Failing test/log before + passing after * [x] Trace/log snippets * [x] Screenshot/recording * [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What I personally verified (not just CI), and how: * Verified scenarios: * Built the Linux app from a clean checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME. * Ran the Meson test suite locally. * Verified tray helper spawn and tray/status behavior. * Verified systemd user-service discovery and lifecycle reflection. * Verified diagnostics/readiness/runtime presentation. * Verified first-run progression manually with screenshots: * app launched before `openclaw setup` * app after `openclaw setup` * app after gateway install/start with the gateway answering properly * Verified the healthy connected state manually against a fresh configured local gateway. * Verified additional Linux app surfaces manually, including Dashboard, General, and Diagnostics. * Verified the main Linux companion window and controller-backed sections load and refresh. * Verified native management flows across Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances at the implementation level. * Verified config editing flow behavior, including full-document rebuild and hash-aware save path. * Verified locality-aware log/transcript fallback behavior. * Edge cases checked: * Invalid config JSON aborts save. * Config rebuild preserves unrelated top-level keys and sibling channel config. * Mutation request shapes match the intended backend contract structure. * RPC lifecycle handling covers success/error/timeout/disconnect cleanup paths. * Tray-helper lifecycle and cleanup behavior were tightened. * Readiness states remain intelligible across the bootstrap sequence from no setup -> configured -> installed/running. * What I did **not** verify: * Official distro packaging artifacts (`.deb`, `.rpm`, Snap) are not included here. * Broad desktop-environment compatibility beyond the Ubuntu GNOME target path. * Full end-to-end parity for every macOS companion capability. ## Review Conversations * [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. * [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration * Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) * No * Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) * No * If yes, exact upgrade steps: * N/A ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. * Risk: * Linux desktop behavior can vary by distro and shell, especially around tray hosting. * Mitigation: * Target the initial implementation clearly at Ubuntu GNOME, use a private AppIndicator helper, and keep the helper boundary isolated from the GTK4 main process. * Risk: * The Linux companion now depends on a larger native runtime stack: systemd context, HTTP health, WebSocket RPC, and gateway payload parsing. * Mitigation: * Keep those concerns separated into dedicated modules and cover parser/RPC/mutation behavior with focused tests. * Risk: * Config editing could accidentally overwrite unrelated config content if the save path is too narrow. * Mitigation: * Load the full config document, edit only the relevant subtree, rebuild the full document, validate JSON before save, and send `baseHash` with `config.set`. * Risk: * Transcript/log opening can fail or behave incorrectly if the gateway resource is not local to the GTK host. * Mitigation: * Enforce locality-aware fallback logic and only open local resources directly when they are actually present and accessible. * Risk: * This is a large first-class app addition and therefore a large review surface. * Mitigation: * Keep the app organized into focused subsystems and section controllers, provide a documented Linux build/test path, and include targeted tests for the most contract-sensitive areas.

target.title: fix(cron): pass allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent from cron executor for both runner paths
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94224: cron jobs that intentionally produce empty replies (silent alert watchers — "watch X, say nothing unless there is something to report") fail every run with `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response` even when the underlying runner returns success.

Supersedes PR #94491 which used a lane substring heuristic in the shared CLI runner. Per ClawSweeper review, the correct boundary is the cron executor.

## Root cause

The `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` flag is only computed for DM/group chat contexts. The cron executor never sets it, so both the CLI and embedded runner paths throw on empty output.

## Changes

In `src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts`: set `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` on both runner calls:

1. **CLI path** (`runCliAgent` call): added flag
2. **Embedded path** (`runEmbeddedAgent` call): added flag

```typescript
// Intentionally-silent cron jobs (watchers that say nothing unless
// there is something to report) should not throw empty_response.
allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true,
```

This is a +6 line change to a single file.

## Why this is better than PR #94491

| PR #94491 (old) | This PR (new) |
|----------------|---------------|
| Lane substring check in shared `cli-runner.ts` | Flag set at cron executor boundary |
| Only covers CLI runner path | Covers CLI + embedded runner paths |
| Broadens shared guard for any lane containing "cron" | No shared guard change |
| `!String(lane).includes("cron")` heuristic | Explicit `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Cron jobs with intentionally empty replies fail with `empty_response` error on both CLI and embedded runner paths.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24, full repository checkout
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```shell
  $ node /tmp/proof-94491-v2.mjs
  ```
  The script verifies the flag propagation for both CLI and embedded paths, DM/chat non-regression, message tool delivery, and text output delivery.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output:
  ```
  ======================================================================
  PR #94491 v2 Live Proof — cron executor allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent
  ======================================================================

  Case 1: CLI runner — cron silent watcher (empty reply)
    result: ok (no throw)
    status: PASS

  Case 2: Embedded runner — cron silent watcher (empty reply)
    result: ok (no throw)
    status: PASS

  Case 3: DM chat (no flag) — empty reply still throws
    result: FailoverError: empty_response
    status: PASS (correctly throws)

  Case 4: Cron job with actual text — normal delivery
    result: All systems nominal.
    status: PASS

  Case 5: Cron job using message tool — delivery handled by tool
    result: ok (message tool handled delivery)
    status: PASS

  Case 6: Verification — executor passes flag to BOTH paths
    CLI path has flag: YES
    Embedded path has flag: YES
    Both paths covered: PASS

  ======================================================================
  RESULTS: 6/6 scenarios pass
  ======================================================================
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: Both CLI and embedded cron paths accept empty replies without throwing. DM/group chat contexts are unchanged (still throw on empty replies unless their own `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` is set).
- **What was not tested**: Live cron job running on a scheduled Gateway (requires Gateway with cron configuration)

## Related

- PR #94491: original lane-heuristic approach (superseded)
- PR #94235: competing executor implementation for same issue

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(doctor): add Node.js runtime info health contribution
target.body:
## Summary
Add a new Doctor health contribution that displays Node.js runtime
diagnostics during `openclaw doctor`, providing essential context for
troubleshooting and proactive upgrade guidance.
## What it does
**Runtime summary note** (always shown):
Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
**Version manager detection** — identifies nvm, fnm, volta, asdf, n,
nodenv, nodebrew, and nvs from the executable path.
**Proactive warnings** (when applicable):
- Node version below minimum (>=22.19.0) — rarely triggers since the
  startup gate catches this first, but guards edge cases
- Node release in maintenance phase — shows months remaining until EOL
- Node release past end-of-life — recommends a current Active LTS release
- Older-than-recommended LTS — gentle nudge toward the recommended LTS
## Why
Doctor currently has no visibility into the Node.js runtime environment.
When users share Doctor output in issues or with maintainers, the Node
version and installation method are critical diagnostic context that was
previously missing.
This reuses existing infrastructure:
- `runtime-guard.ts` — `detectRuntime()`, `parseSemver()`, `runtimeSatisfies()`
- `runtime-paths.ts` — `isVersionManagedNodePath()`
No new dependencies. No runtime behavior changes outside of Doctor.
## Changes
New files:
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.ts` (~200 lines) — diagnostics
  collection, warning generation, summary formatting, contribution entry
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.test.ts` — 27 unit tests
Modified:
- `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts` — +1 import, +1 wrapper
  function, +1 contribution registration (after `bundled-plugin-runtime-deps`,
  before `state-integrity`)
## Test plan
- `pnpm check` — 0 errors ✅
- `vitest` — 27/27 tests ✅
- `pnpm build` — clean ✅
Test coverage:
- `detectVersionManagerName`: 11 tests (null, system, nvm, fnm, volta,
  asdf, n, nodenv, nodebrew, nvs, Windows backslash)
- `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics`: 3 tests (nvm path, unknown runtime,
  system install)
- `buildNodeRuntimeWarnings`: 8 tests (recommended version, below
  minimum, past EOL, maintenance phase, pre-maintenance nudge, current
  recommended, null major, future major)
- `buildNodeRuntimeSummary`: 5 tests (system install, named manager,
  unknown manager, null version, separator format)
---
## Real behavior proof
_Refreshed for current head `f90eee0a` (after the Windows case-insensitive OS-home redaction commit)._

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The Doctor Node runtime note renders the executable path via a two-pass redaction (`shortenHomePath` for the OpenClaw home, then an OS-home pass). The OS-home pass compared the Windows home prefix with exact casing, so a valid Windows path whose drive/username casing differed from `os.homedir()` (e.g. `os.homedir()` = `C:\Users\Alice`, `execPath` = `c:\users\alice\...`) fell through unredacted and leaked the OS username. POSIX comparison stays case-sensitive; the Windows backslash-boundary prefix is now compared case-insensitively, slicing by the original length to preserve real casing.

- **Real environment tested:** Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js 24.14.0, current PR head `f90eee0a`. Invoked the real `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics()` + `buildNodeRuntimeSummary()` — the same render `noteNodeRuntime()` emits in the Doctor health flow — plus injected lifecycle scenarios via the exported helpers.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran the registered render path via tsx against the real runtime:
```bash
$ node --import tsx run-node-runtime-note.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix (current head):** Block 1 is the real render on this machine. Block 2 is the POSIX OS-home redaction scenario (OPENCLAW_HOME elsewhere). Block 3 is the new Windows case-insensitive redaction this commit fixes. Remaining blocks are injected lifecycle scenarios against the current date.
```
=== 1. Real current-head render (Node v24.14.0) ===
Node 24.14.0 · /usr/bin/node · system install

=== 2. POSIX OS-home redaction (OPENCLAW_HOME=/srv/openclaw, Node under the user's OS home) ===
os home (HOME)     : /home/<user>
input  execPath    : /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node
output rendered    : Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
leaks OS username? : NO (redacted)

=== 3. Windows case-insensitive redaction (the new fix) ===
os.homedir()       : C:\Users\Alice   (canonical casing)
input  execPath    : c:\users\alice\AppData\Roaming\nvm\v24.14.0\node.exe   (lower-cased)
output rendered    : Node 24.14.0 · ~\AppData\Roaming\nvm\v24.14.0\node.exe · via nvm
leaks OS username? : NO (redacted)

=== Injected Node 22 LTS (22.19.0, in maintenance) ===
Node 22 LTS is in maintenance mode (EOL 2027-04-30, ~10 months remaining).
Consider upgrading to Node 24 for the latest features and longer support.

=== Injected Node 20 (20.18.0, below the >=22.19.0 minimum) ===
Node 20.18.0 does not meet the minimum requirement (>=22.19.0).
Upgrade Node: https://nodejs.org/en/download
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The real Node 24.14.0 runtime renders a clean note (supported, system install). Both the POSIX OS-home scenario (block 2) and the Windows case-mismatch scenario (block 3) now redact the home to `~`, so no OS username appears — including when `execPath` casing differs from `os.homedir()`. Before this commit, the exact-case Windows prefix check left such paths unredacted. Injected Node 22 LTS / 20 produce the expected maintenance / below-minimum advisories. POSIX, Windows OPENCLAW_HOME-override, and the new Windows case-mismatch regression tests cover the redaction paths; 51 unit tests pass, types and lint clean.

- **What was not tested:** The full interactive `openclaw doctor` TUI run was not captured (it triggers a heavy dist build); the registered render path is invoked directly instead. Redaction relies on `os.homedir()`; in the rare case where `HOME`/`USERPROFILE` is unset and differs from the path's owner (e.g. some sudo/container setups), redaction may not apply — out of scope for this detect-only diagnostic.

target.title: fix: prevent silent message loss from EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError
target.body:
## Summary

When the OpenAI SDK retried an `ECONNRESET` internally (default `maxRetries=2`), the session write lock was released during the retry window. Incoming steering messages could then modify the transcript without updating the fence, causing a fingerprint mismatch on `reacquireAfterPrompt()`. The resulting `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` was caught by `model-fallback.ts` which aborted the run, and the outer reply pipeline produced no user-visible output — the message was silently lost.

## Root Cause Chain

```
API ECONNRESET
  → OpenAI SDK default maxRetries=2, internal retry
  → session write lock released during retry window
  → incoming message arrives via queueHandle.queueMessage
  → activeSession.steer writes directly (bypassing withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites)
  → transcript changes without updating fence
  → original attempt reacquireAfterPrompt() fingerprint mismatch
  → EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError thrown
  → model-fallback.ts aborts the run
  → outer layer hits generic failure branch
  → assistantTexts/payloads never enter buildReplyPayloads()
  → message silently lost (user sees generic error or nothing)
```

## Fix

Five coordinated changes that address the bug at multiple layers:

### 1. Wrap steering in `withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites` (`attempt.ts`)
Steering writes that arrive during prompt lock release now go through `withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites`, keeping fence tracking consistent and preventing the fingerprint mismatch that triggers the takeover error.

### 2. Dedicated error handler for takeover (`agent-runner-execution.ts`, `failover-error.ts`)
Added `isEmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError()` export and a dedicated catch block in `runAgentTurnWithFallback`. Instead of falling into the generic failure branch (silent loss), users now receive a clear message asking them to resend. When a takeover wraps a preserved prompt error (billing, rate-limit, context-overflow), the original error is unwrapped and classified normally — only pure takeover errors return resend guidance.

### 3. Scope SDK retry disable to embedded prompt lock path (`attempt.ts`, `attempt.session-lock.ts`)
Extracted `installEmbeddedPromptRetryDefault` helper that wraps the session's `streamFn` with `{ maxRetries: 0, ...options }` — the `0` is a **default** that goes before the caller options spread, so `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries` (surfaced via `options.maxRetries` per the chain in `sdk.ts:360`) takes precedence when explicitly configured. Non-embedded callers retain the SDK default retry behavior (maxRetries=2).

### 4. Forward maxRetries through native OpenAI transport (`openai-transport-stream.ts`)
`buildOpenAISdkRequestOptions` previously accepted only `model` and `signal`, dropping the `maxRetries` injected by `installEmbeddedPromptRetryDefault`. Added `maxRetries` parameter and forwarded it through all three native transport call sites (Responses, Azure Responses, Completions). When `maxRetries` is the only option present, the function now returns an object instead of `undefined` so the SDK receives the override.

### 5. Phase tracking in `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` (`attempt.session-lock.ts`)
Added a `phase` field (`prompt_reacquire`, `cleanup`, `write`, `fence_check`) for better diagnostics when this error does occur. Backward compatible — the phase parameter is optional with existing call sites unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

> **Refreshed at current head `bf4c277c9b` (supersedes the v6 proof below, which targeted older head `2fc3ecee2d`).**

- **Behavior addressed**: A session takeover during the embedded prompt-lock release window must not silently drop the user's reply, and the injected embedded retry default must refresh with the latest configured `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries` across repeated installs (not freeze the first-captured value).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `bf4c277c9b`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, freshly installed deps (`pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran the affected suites at this head with `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs <suite>` — the organic session-takeover regression (real on-disk session + real local HTTP model server + real OpenAI-completions transport + real prompt-lock fence), the retry-default live-ref unit suite (V1–V5), and the reply-runtime regression — plus `tsgo:test:src` for typecheck.
- **Evidence after fix**: Copied live terminal output captured at head `bf4c277c9b`:
```
 ✓ surfaces resend guidance after an organic EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError  724ms
 ✓ src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts (72 tests) 141ms
      Tests  72 passed (72)
      Tests  205 passed (205)
 tsgo:test:src exits 0 with no errors
```
- **Observed result after fix**: The organic session takeover now surfaces explicit resend guidance ("⚠️ Your message was interrupted because new input arrived while the model was retrying a connection error. Please resend your message.") instead of silently dropping the reply; the retry-default wrapper reads the shared live ref at call time so a repeat install refreshes the next call (including downward refresh when the configured value drops); and typecheck is clean at this head.
- **What was not tested**: The live Feishu/DeepSeek ECONNRESET timing race that originally surfaced #87180 is not reproduced here; the takeover is reproduced deterministically via a real on-disk fence-drift, which exercises the same `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` path. The CI matrix on this head was still running at proof time.


### Current-head proof (`bf4c277c9b`)

- **Behavior addressed**: A session takeover during the embedded prompt-lock release window must not silently drop the user's reply, and the injected embedded retry default must refresh with the latest configured `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries` across repeated installs (not freeze the first-captured value).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `bf4c277c9b`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, freshly installed deps (`pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`).
- **Why refreshed**: The previous proof targeted an older head; the retry-default wrapper was reworked to read a shared live ref at call time (commit on this head), so proof is regenerated on the resulting head.

### 1. Organic session-takeover regression (real on-disk session + real local HTTP model server + real OpenAI-completions transport + real prompt-lock fence)
```
     ✓ surfaces resend guidance after an organic EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError  724ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed (1)
```
The takeover error is thrown organically by the real prompt-lock fence (a concurrent steering write rewrites the same session file mid-call), and `runAgentTurnWithFallback` surfaces resend guidance instead of dropping the reply.

### 2. Retry-default live-ref unit proof (the wrapper fix, V1–V5)
```
 ✓  agents  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts (72 tests) 141ms
      Tests  72 passed (72)
```
Covers: refresh through the buried wrapper after lock-release wraps it; call-time live read so a repeat install changes the next call; downward refresh when the configured value drops (the original bug's essence); shared ref identity across both install orders; and no wrapper stacking on repeated installs.

### 3. Reply-runtime regression (no behavior regression on the takeover catch path)
```
      Tests  205 passed (205)
```

### Typecheck
`tsgo:test:src` exits 0 with no errors at this head.

### What was not tested
The live Feishu/DeepSeek ECONNRESET timing race that originally surfaced #87180 is not reproduced here; the takeover is reproduced deterministically via a real on-disk fence-drift, which exercises the same `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` path. The CI matrix on this head was still running at proof time.


<details>
<summary>Earlier proof (v6, head 2fc3ecee2d) — historical</summary>


- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Silent message loss on session takeover during the released prompt-lock window. When the OpenAI SDK retried an `ECONNRESET` internally, an incoming steering write changed the transcript without updating the fence, raising `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError`, which `model-fallback.ts` aborted into a generic failure branch so the user saw no actionable output. After this fix the pure-takeover path returns explicit resend guidance, the SDK retry default is scoped to the embedded prompt-lock window (with configured `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries` taking precedence), and `maxRetries` is forwarded through the native OpenAI transport.
- **Real environment tested**: Clean Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (Node.js `v24.16.0`, Debian GNU/Linux 12 bookworm, aarch64) running the patched checkout at the current head commit `2fc3ecee2d`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Built the patched checkout in the container and ran the affected suites plus a real-setup organic takeover test (real on-disk session file + real local `node:http` model server on an `openai-completions` baseUrl + real transport + real fence) and code-path verification, as captured in the v6 proof log below.
- **Evidence after fix**: Copied live terminal output from the patched container at head `2fc3ecee2d` (full v6 log reproduced in the section below):

```

AFTER-fix user-facing payload text (exact):

BEFORE-fix user-facing payload text (exact, parent 86150a3e51):
⚠️ Something went wrong while processing your request. Please try again, or use /new to start a fresh session.
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The organic session takeover now surfaces a specific, actionable resend message (the after-fix payload line above) instead of the generic non-actionable failure seen at the parent; the configured provider retry survives through `sdk.ts` to the native SDK request options; the native transport forwards `maxRetries`; and the retry-default installer is idempotent with caller > configured > 0 precedence.
- **What was not tested**: No live multi-user deployment against a real upstream provider with an actual network-level `ECONNRESET` was run. The takeover proof uses a real on-disk session file, a real local HTTP model server, the real transport, and the real fence (organic error), with only the upstream provider socket and the "second user" steering write synthesized; the supplementary transport proof relies on unit/loopback tests in a real Docker container.

### Current-head proof (commit `2fc3ecee2d`, Docker `node:24-bookworm`)

Refreshed at the latest head, which adds the configured-provider-retry precedence. Run in a clean Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`, Node.js `v24.16.0`, Debian GNU/Linux 12 bookworm, aarch64):

```
Install site passes configured provider retry:
```
